summary:
The ACES Act of 2025 directs the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to arrange for the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to study cancer prevalence and mortality among certain former active duty military aircrew members.

The Act requires the Secretary, within 30 days of enactment, to seek an agreement with the National Academies to conduct the study. The Secretary must finalize the agreement within 60 days after entering negotiations. If that deadline is not met, the Secretary must report to the Senate and House Veterans’ Affairs Committees explaining the delay and estimating when the agreement will be finalized, and must provide those committees progress briefings at least every 60 days until finalization.

The required study must identify exposures associated with the military occupations of covered aircrew members, including chemicals, compounds, agents, and other phenomena. It must review scientific literature on associations between those exposures and overall cancer morbidity and mortality, as well as increased incidence or prevalence of specified cancers, including brain, colon and rectal, kidney, lung, melanoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, pancreatic, prostate, testicular, thyroid, and urinary bladder cancers, plus any other cancers the Secretary considers appropriate in consultation with the National Academies.

The study must also determine, to the extent possible, the prevalence of and mortality from those cancers among covered individuals using available data sources, which may include Department of Veterans Affairs, Department of Defense, and military service databases; the National Death Index; and a prior study required by the fiscal year 2021 National Defense Authorization Act.

At the conclusion of the study, the National Academies must submit a report with the study results to the Secretary and the House and Senate Veterans’ Affairs Committees. The Act defines covered individuals as those who served on active duty in the Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps as fixed-wing aircraft aircrew, including pilots, navigators, weapons systems operators, aircraft system operators, and other crew members who regularly flew in fixed-wing aircraft.
